3A_Bibliographic.citation: Tapert, S. F., Reilly, E. B., Engel, S., Jacobus, J., Huestis, M. A., & Squeglia, L. M. (2024). Risk assessment of maladaptive behaviors in adolescents: Nutrition, screen time, prenatal exposure, childhood adversities - Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Journal of Adolescent Health, 74(3), 515-525.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Screen time is one of several risk factors in a composite variable; not the primary exposure in isolation.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Of a total 11,878 participants, the sample size was reduced to 6,830 in our analysis based on the amount of available data for total fat and sugar intake. A total of 6,830 children were randomly divided into two sets of equal size: the test set and the validation set, with each set consisting of 3,415 children.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Test set n=3415, validation set n=3415. Total analytic sample is 6830 split into two.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time (weekday and weekend) as one component of a composite risk variable alongside nutrition, prenatal exposure, parental behavior, family environment, and SES
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (z-scored and included in composite risk variable along with other factors)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time usage... Factor 5 (Screen time exposure) Weekday; weekend... Weekday screen time [factor loading] 0.68512... Weekend screen time 0.65903
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is embedded in a composite risk score with 9 other variables. Not examined as an isolated predictor.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Reward sensitivity (BIS/BAS), impulsivity (UPPS-P), behavioral problems (CBCL), and brain activation during MID task
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: BIS/BAS scales, UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale, Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL), MID task-based fMRI
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: We used the BAS drive, fun seeking, reward responsiveness, and BIS sum scores, as well as the UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale and Child Behavior. These measures were collected at the 2-year follow-up event.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains: brain activation, behavioral/mental health measures. Primary focus is on brain reward processing.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Exploratory factor analysis to create composite risk variable, then linear mixed effects models with site as random effect to compare risk groups on behavioral and neural outcomes at 2-year follow-up
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, study site (random effect)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We employed linear mixed effect models to examine the association between risk groups and behavioral as well as neural changes in children at the 2-year follow-up... To account for multiple pairwise comparisons, we applied a Bonferroni correction with an adjusted alpha level of 0.05/3 = 0.0167

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: High-risk group (includes screen time) vs low-risk group
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: high-risk children (vs. low-risk) revealed significantly stronger associations with elevated BAS drive [test set (beta = 0.38; 95% CI = 0.12, 0.64; p = .004)]
negative urgency scores [test set (beta = 0.65; 95% CI = 0.42, 0.87; p < .001)]
positive urgency scores [test set (beta = 0.98; 95% CI = 0.73, 1.24; p < .001)]

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Caudal MFC activation during reward vs no-incentive anticipation (MID task)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Beta estimate from MID task fMRI
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: caudal MFC (test set, beta = -0.02; 95% CI = -0.04, -0.004; p = .01)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Thalamus activation during reward vs no-incentive anticipation (MID task)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Beta estimate from MID task fMRI

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Insula activation during loss avoidance vs no-incentive anticipation
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Beta estimate from MID task fMRI
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: high-risk (vs. low-risk) children in both test and validation set were associated with lower insular activation [(test set, beta = -0.02; 95% CI = -0.03, -0.004; p = .01)]
caudal MFC (test set, beta = -0.02; 95% CI = -0.002, 0.03; p = .02)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: The effect size magnitudes for our brain findings were relatively small, which most likely pertains to the significant differences in our risk groups (p < .001) in many regards (sex and race/ethnicity). The small effect sizes seen in our and other studies using this large ABCD data set may be due to limitations of the sample
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper acknowledges small effect sizes for brain findings but still recommends clinical interventions and risk identification. The behavioral effect sizes (e.g., beta=0.98 for positive urgency) are moderate.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: This study has implications for early risk identification and the development of prevention strategies for maladaptive behaviors in children, particularly those at high risk.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: The identified risk factors may be potentially malleable and future target for intervention, opening avenues for future research to confirm our findings.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion interprets the composite risk group differences in brain activation and behavior as evidence that screen time combined with other adversity factors contribute to altered reward processing and impulsivity. The authors frame findings within addiction risk models and recommend early identification and prevention, though screen time is not isolated as an independent predictor.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is bundled into a composite risk variable with nutrition, prenatal exposure, family environment, and SES. It is impossible to isolate the specific contribution of screen time to outcomes. The primary screen-time-to-outcome association is confounded by design.
